title: The Seventh Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Sermon: "The Seventh Sunday after Pentecost" | Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver
Date: July 19, 2020 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Seventh Sunday after Pentecost, placing the church in Ordinary Time (the long season between Pentecost and Advent). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings for this Sunday likely included Genesis 28:10-19a (Jacob's ladder dream), Psalm 139:1-12, 23-24, Romans 8:12-25, and Matthew 13:24-30, 36-43 (parable of the wheat and tares). The season of Ordinary Time emphasizes discipleship, growth, and living out the Gospel in everyday life, contrasting with the more penitential seasons.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating significant internal tensions in mid-2020 regarding racial justice, prayer book revision discussions, and responses to the COVID-19 pandemic that had forced churches to suspend in-person worship since March. The Diocese of Colorado was among many dioceses grappling with how to safely reopen churches while honoring public health guidance; some congregations had begun limited in-person services by this date while others remained fully virtual. Broderick Greer, as a prominent Black Episcopal priest and theologian, was engaged in broader church conversations about racism and justice that had intensified following George Floyd's death on May 25, 2020.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the thick of summer 2020 with ongoing racial justice protests and demonstrations following the national uprising after George Floyd's murder; local activism had been sustained for nearly two months with regular marches downtown and in Capitol Hill. The Denver Police Department faced significant public scrutiny and calls for reform amid broader "defund the police" conversations. Colorado's COVID-19 case numbers were rising in mid-July (the state was approaching 40,000 total cases), and mask mandates remained in effect despite some reopening efforts.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The U.S. was in the midst of a severe COVID-19 surge, with cases climbing sharply across the South and West; Florida, Texas, and Arizona were reporting record hospitalizations. The November 2020 presidential election was roughly four months away, with campaigns intensifying and the Democratic National Convention scheduled to begin on August 17. The debate over school reopening for fall 2020 was reaching a crescendo, with President Trump threatening to cut federal funding to schools that didn't reopen full-time while epidemiologists warned against premature reopening.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Hong Kong was under intense strain following China's passage of the National Security Law on June 30, with pro-democracy protesters mobilizing and international condemnation (including from the U.S.) mounting through mid-July. Brazil's COVID-19 crisis was severe and worsening, with the country approaching 80,000 deaths and President Jair Bolsonaro continuing to downplay the pandemic despite his own positive test on July 7. India and China were in a military standoff in the Himalayas following border clashes in June that had killed at least 20 Indian soldiers.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The summer of 2020 was marked by deep national reckoning on racism, police brutality, and systemic inequality, with "Black Lives Matter" becoming a dominant cultural and political phrase. The mood was simultaneously urgent and fractured—energized by protest movements but exhausted by pandemic isolation, economic uncertainty (unemployment remained elevated from COVID shutdowns), and political polarization heading into a contentious election season. Sports were attempting return (MLB, NBA, and NFL were planning resumed seasons with COVID protocols), and streaming entertainment consumption remained elevated due to lockdowns and social distancing.
